title: Some results on history dependent stochastic processes
speaker: Margherita Disertori
abstract: Edge reinforced random walk (ERRW) and vertex reinforced jump processes are history dependent stochastic process, where the particle tends to come back more often on sites it has already visited in the past. For a particular scheme of reinforcement these processes are mixtures of reversible Markov chains whose mixing measure can be related to a non-linear sigma model introduced in the context of random matrices. I will give an overview on these models and explain some recent results in joint work with F. Merkl and S. Rolles.

proof_stages.label: Reinforced random walk setup and strength parameters
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces history-dependent stochastic processes on graphs, defines once-reinforced and linearly edge-reinforced random walks, and explains that small initial edge weights correspond to strong reinforcement while large weights give weak reinforcement.

proof_stages.label: Mixture representation for linearly edge-reinforced walks
proof_stages.summary: The linearly edge-reinforced random walk is represented, on finite graphs, as a mixture of reversible Markov chains in frozen random edge-weight environments, with a mixing measure independent of the particular finite-time event.

proof_stages.label: Known phase diagram and sigma-model method for LERRW
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states known localization, recurrence, and transience results for linearly edge-reinforced walks in various dimensions and explains that recent progress uses the connection between the mixing measure and a nonlinear sigma model, with alternative proofs still relying on the mixing representation.

proof_stages.label: Vertex-reinforced jump process and its relation to LERRW
proof_stages.summary: The continuous-time vertex-reinforced jump process is defined via jump rates reinforced by target local times, then related by Sabot and Tarrès to mixtures of reversible Markov chains with weights W_ij = beta_ij exp(T_i+T_j), whose gamma averaging over beta recovers the linearly edge-reinforced walk.

proof_stages.label: VRJP consequences, open regimes, and strip theorem
proof_stages.summary: Existing sigma-model estimates yield VRJP localization and transience results in strong and weak reinforcement regimes, leaving intermediate and two-dimensional cases open, and the speaker states the new theorem of exponential localization on finite-width strips for all beta.

proof_stages.label: Tilted-measure variational reduction
proof_stages.summary: To prove exponential localization, the relevant weight ratio is reduced to an expectation of exp((T_l-T_0)/2), absorbed into a tilted measure, and bounded through a variational or entropy inequality by choosing a deformed probability measure with controlled entropy cost and negative observable contribution.

proof_stages.label: Adapted strip deformation and transfer-matrix control
proof_stages.summary: The talk contrasts the Merkl-Rolles deformation method for two-dimensional edge-reinforced walks with the unbounded-error problem for VRJP, then describes the adapted deformation and transfer-matrix estimates that work in quasi-one-dimensional strip geometry.

examples.name: Unknown-city walker
examples.note: An everyday walker who first moves randomly in an unfamiliar city and later prefers previously visited streets illustrates history dependence before the formal reinforced-walk definitions.
examples.engagement_reason: the unknown-city walker is used only as an intuitive analogy for history-dependent behavior, with no concrete calculation or construction carried out on it

passes.reason: A generic informal history-dependent walker used as motivation, with no special intrinsic structure.
An unknown-city walker is just an informal representative history-dependent/reinforced walk analogy, with no special structural status.

examples.name: Cylinder and hitting events for the event A
examples.note: In response to a question about the set A, the speaker gives fixed finite paths and boundary-hitting-before-return events as typical events whose probabilities are averaged over frozen environments.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker discusses event A concretely as a cylinder/path or hitting event and explains how its probability in a frozen environment is obtained, e.g. as a product of jumping rates for a specified path event

examples.name: Six-step and ten-step edge-crossing queries
examples.note: An audience member asks about crossing a given edge after six versus ten steps, prompting clarification that the frozen-environment probability changes with the event while the mixing measure itself does not depend on time.
examples.engagement_reason: the six-step and ten-step edge-crossing questions are worked through to explain that the mixing measure stays the same while the frozen-environment event probability changes with the time/event

passes.reason: Fixed-time edge-crossing queries are standard finite-time events used to clarify the formula, not to refute it.

examples.name: Reinforced walks on trees
examples.note: Trees are mentioned as a contrasting graph family where recurrence-transience transitions can be proved, illustrating how the phase diagram depends on the underlying graph.
examples.engagement_reason: reinforced walks on trees are only cited as a setting where recurrence/transience transition results are known, without deriving or checking anything on trees

passes.reason: Trees form the acyclic/minimally connected extreme among connected graphs, used as a tractable graph family rather than a counterexample.
Reinforced walks on trees use tree graphs, which are minimally connected/acyclic extremal graphs among connected graphs, as a tractable setting for recurrence-transience results.
Trees are acyclic/minimally connected underlying graphs, used as a special setting where recurrence-transience results are known.
tree-based walks use acyclic minimally connected graphs, and are cited as a tractable setting rather than a refutation

Walks on trees use acyclic minimally connected graph domains, a sparse boundary case cited for known recurrence/transience results rather than as a counterexample.
Reinforced walks on trees use tree graphs, a systematic acyclic/minimally connected extreme among graphs, as a tractable setting.
Walks on trees use acyclic minimally connected graph structures, an extremal class among connected graphs, but not a counterexample.
Walks on trees are used as another setting with recurrence/transience results, and trees are acyclic minimally connected graphs.

Trees form the minimally connected acyclic boundary case among connected graphs, used here as a setting where recurrence/transience transitions can be proved.
Reinforced walks on trees use tree graphs, an acyclic minimally connected extremal graph family, as a setting where recurrence/transience transitions are known.
